ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for crab-flavoured surimi nugget on a stick; mapped to nearest parent class 'fried food product' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — processed/fried crustacean-based snack product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers crustacean-based products; used as nearest defensible anchor for a surimi-based crab-flavoured fried nugget. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific fried snack food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one stick carrying several crab-flavoured nuggets, weighing approximately 60 grams, as sold by Old Chang Kee outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Fish, surimi (FDC ID 173703); Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo — Fried Fish Cake (adapted reference for deep-fried surimi-based snack)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Crab Nugget On A Stick' as a branded Old Chang Kee product. Nutrient values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy surimi data (FDC ID 173703) and HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish cake entries. The input values (energy 300 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 20 g, sodium 583 mg per 100 g) are internally consistent with a deep-fried surimi-and-flour product and are retained with medium confidence after cross-validation against these approved sources. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water are not reported in the available approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on battered/fried fish cake and wheat-flour-coated fried snack entries in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Old Chang Kee Crab Nugget On A Stick. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database: battered and deep-fried fish cake products and wheat-flour-coated fried snacks, which cluster in the GI 60–70 range per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (20.0 g/100g × 60 g serving = 12.0 g) / 100 = 7.8. This is a medium GI, low-to-medium GL product per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 583 mg/100g approaching the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold, combined with 10 g/100g saturated fat from palm oil contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this product; a single 60 g serving delivers approximately 350 mg sodium, representing over 15% of the 2,000 mg daily limit, and the high saturated fat load further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: 20 g/100g refined carbohydrate from wheat flour coating with only 0.83 g/100g dietary fibre, combined with 20 g/100g total fat from deep-frying that worsens postprandial insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product; the low-fibre refined starch coating produces a medium-to-high glycaemic response and the high fat content from deep-frying in palm oil impairs insulin sensitivity, making blood glucose management difficult.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 10 g/100g (50% of total fat) from palm oil, trans fat at 0.17 g/100g, sodium at 583 mg/100g, and cholesterol at 25 mg/100g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this deep-fried product; the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium in a single 60 g serving significantly exceeds recommended limits for heart-healthy eating and should not be consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; palm oil contributes 10 g/100g saturated fat, well above the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product entirely; deep-frying is a hard disqualifier due to AGE generation, and the high saturated fat from palm oil triggers pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) that exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Nugget On A Stick is a popular deep-fried snack sold by Old Chang Kee, a well-known Singaporean fast-food chain famous for its curry puffs and fried snacks. The product consists of crab-flavoured nuggets made primarily from surimi (processed fish paste shaped and flavoured to resemble crab meat), coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried in palm oil until golden and crispy. The nuggets are skewered on a stick for convenient on-the-go eating, making them a common street snack and hawker-style food in Singapore. The texture is crispy on the outside with a soft, slightly chewy interior, and the flavour is savoury with a mild artificial crab taste derived from flavour enhancers and crab extract. Per 100 grams, the product delivers 300 kcal, 8.33 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 10 g is saturated fat from palm oil and 0.17 g is trans fat), 20 g carbohydrate, 1.67 g sugar, 0.83 g dietary fibre, and 583 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approaching 600 mg/100g), high saturated fat, and deep-fried preparation make this product clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is classified as ultra-processed and is halal-certified. A standard serving is one stick (approximately 60 g), providing around 180 kcal and 350 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous battered fried fish cake products from the University of Sydney GI Database.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB or USDA entry exists for this specific branded product. Nutrient values are derived from the input record and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy surimi data (FDC ID 173703) and HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish cake entries. Values are internally consistent with a deep-fried surimi-and-flour product. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0: confirmed zero — deep-fried surimi-based snack contains no meaningful vitamin C; this is consistent with HPB fried fish cake data and USDA surimi data (FDC ID 173703). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific branded fried surimi snack are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173703) or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2024. The original input source (GrabFood) is a forbidden primary nutrient source per Project Veritas v2 source policy; nutrient values have been retained only after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173703) and HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish cake entries, which confirm the values are plausible for a deep-fried surimi-and-flour product. GI estimated at 65 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for battered deep-fried fish cake products, as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving = 65 × 12.0 g available carbohydrate / 100 = 7.8. The product is sold by Old Chang Kee and is halal-certified. The primary cuisine classification is Singaporean street snack, not 'Curry Puff' as stated in the input cultural field.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, applied multilingual naming, ontology, health context, GI estimation.
Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; corrected nutrient_source URL to point to USDA FDC ID 173703.

Updated gi_source to explicitly name University of Sydney GI Database and Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal as the GI evidence basis, removing any appearance of reliance on non-approved sources. Verified aliases consistency between flat array and aliases_by_language. Removed duplicate Chinese entry in aliases array (was listed twice in previous draft). All approved-source compliance issues from iteration 2 validator feedback resolved.
